rationale: Authority data for W40K-0141..W40K-0150 — an unusually-split temporal cluster: seven 2025-2026 publication-frontier releases (Voidscarred + Tomb World + Vagabond Squadron + Archmagos + Demolisher + Death Rider + The Green Tide omnibus) plus the **founding Ian-Watson Inquisition-War trilogy from 1990-1995** (Inquisitor / Draco + Harlequin + Chaos Child — the literal first three Warhammer-40,000 novels ever published, Inquisitor (1990) being the genesis novel of the entire Black-Library publishing line). This temporal split is the widest of any authority-layer batch so far: ~35-year publication-spread within a single 10er. **Cross-batch character-continuity peaks again**: three direct sequels to prior authority-layer entries (Archmagos = third Belisarius-Cawl-novel after ssot-w40k-011 W40K-0103 *The Great Work* + ssot-w40k-014 W40K-0134 *Genefather* — completing the canonical Cawl-trilogy in the authority layer; Demolisher = Hadeya-Etsul-book-2 after ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0112 *Steel Tread*, same protagonist, same world Croatoas, same crew; The Green Tide omnibus = aggregation-omnibus that includes two prior-authority-layer entries — *Warboss* in ssot-w40k-013 W40K-0128 + *Catachan Devil* in ssot-w40k-012 W40K-0118 — as its constituent novels, requiring authority-layer-internal-aggregation rather than discrete-entry-treatment). **Seven first-authority-layer-as-primary-protagonist surface forms** (Aeldari Corsairs / Starsplinters in Voidscarred — first authority-layer Aeldari-Corsairs-as-primary-protagonist faction-debut, distinct from prior Aeldari/Eldar/Ynnari appearances which were all Craftworld-side or Ynnari-side; Triarch Praetorian-as-primary-POV in Tomb World — Khemet is female-primary-POV-Necron, second authority-layer Necron-as-primary-protagonist after Indomitus ssot-w40k-011 but first as single-POV-female-Praetorian; 901st Tactical Wing + Valkyrie-Aeronautica-Imperialis-squadron in Vagabond Squadron; Death Korps of Krieg 472nd Siege Regiment + Commissar Valian Hesh in Death Rider — second authority-layer Death-Korps-of-Krieg appearance after ssot-w40k-012's *Krieg* but first with on-page Krieg-Commissar dynamic; Squats-as-on-page-protagonist-retinue-member in Inquisitor / Draco — first authority-layer Squat surface form, retconned in-out of canon for decades and now first appearing in the authority layer via the 1990 founding-novel; Hydra-rogue-Inquisition-cabal in Inquisitor / Draco — first authority-layer conspiracy-Inquisition-faction; Harlequins-of-the-Eldar-as-primary-on-page-faction in Harlequin — second authority-layer Harlequins appearance after ssot-w40k-011 *Ephrael Stern*'s Kyganil-supporting-tier but first as primary on-page faction with Ceremony-of-Cataclysm + Black-Library-of-Chaos register). **Three `data_conflict` flags for missing author fields** in the roster (W40K-0141 Mike Brooks; W40K-0142 Jonathan D. Beer; W40K-0143 Robbie MacNiven; W40K-0146 Rhuairidh James) — actually four — confirmed by WebSearch + Black-Library + Lexicanum + Goodreads + Goonhammer + Warhammer-Community + Frontline-Gaming + At-Boundary's-Edge + Simon-&-Schuster; the 2025-2026-publication-frontier author-missing pattern continues from ssot-w40k-014 (5 missing) — consistent with a 2025-late + 2026-early roster-update lag. Plus one **`data_conflict` flag for omnibus-author** (W40K-0147 The Green Tide is a multi-author anthology, primary editor / curator unclear in the roster); plus **one Andy-Clark author-attribution-confirmation-non-conflict** (W40K-0145 Demolisher = Andy Clark is in roster). **Founding-novel-priority adjustment**: Inquisitor (1990) is the first W40K novel ever, and in the original publication it had numerous discontinuities with later codex canon (e.g., Squats, the Hydra-as-rogue-Inquisition conspiracy, the 'Emperor's Sons' / Star-Children-controversy that develops in Harlequin + Chaos Child); my synopses note these as authoritative-to-the-novel-and-its-trilogy without recanonising them, per Brief-061 surface-form-treue. **WebSearch:** mean=1.0/book, max=1 (10 total searches — one obligatory plot-context search per book). The Cawl-trilogy-completion-arc + Watson-founding-trilogy + Green-Tide-omnibus-aggregation each had strong coverage across Black-Library + Lexicanum + Goodreads + Warhammer-Community + Goonhammer + At-Boundary's-Edge + Wikipedia + Ian-Watson's-author-site, so no follow-up searches were needed. **Resolver-Pre-Note**: cumulativeBefore=140 → cumulativeAfter=150; **next iteration triggers the natural Resolver-Pause-window at cumulative=150**. The third Resolver-Brief-trigger will land at the *next* iteration's pre-check (cumulativeBefore=150 % 50 == 0 → loud-stop unless skip-50-stop marker is set). Brief-061 Cowork-tendency from earlier passes recommends writing the Resolver-Brief at that pause rather than deferring further; the surface-form-pile since the post-069 second-window now spans ssot-w40k-006..015 = ~100 Bücher with the founding-Watson-trilogy adding a distinct historical-canon layer (Squats / Hydra / Emperor's-Sons-controversy) that resolver work will need to address separately from the modern-canon surface forms.

overrides.synopsis: Baron Myrin Stormdawn, ageing chieftain of the Starsplinters, has spent centuries choosing the Aeldari Corsair's free and dangerous third path between craftworld discipline and Drukhari cruelty. When an Ork Freebooter klan threatens his raiding grounds, Stormdawn must bind his Corsairs to Taenar Leotharan, an exiled craftworld admiral whose own disgrace mirrors the Corsairs' restless pride. The result is a void-war about freedom, duty, old debts, and the price of living outside any safe path. A melancholic Aeldari naval adventure with Ork violence snapping at its heels.

overrides.synopsis: Khemet, a disgraced Triarch Praetorian, has endured a Necron punishment worse than death: conscious imprisonment for a failure that may not be hers alone. Lord Hekasun offers her one chance to restore her honour by awakening Orymous, a tomb world now paved over and occupied by the Astra Militarum. To complete the mission she must slip between Imperial defences, buried Necron systems, and Hekasun's own dynastic designs. A cold redemption-thriller where immortal pride meets the practical problem of humans living on top of your grave.

overrides.synopsis: The 901st Tactical Wing, better known as Vagabond Squadron, flies Valkyrie assault craft in the dirty close-support war that more glamorous pilots prefer to mock. Sent to Kanai Tertius as Ork air power rises over a collapsing Imperial front, the squadron has to keep troops moving, extractions open, and a novice aviator alive under pressure. Robbie MacNiven turns the air war into a cockpit-level ensemble piece: hot engines, chain-of-command friction, and transport pilots proving that survival often depends on the craft everyone else overlooks.

flags.reason: Roster authors[] empty; canonical author confirmed by Black Library + Lexicanum + At-Boundary's-Edge + Warhammer-Community + Noble Knight Games

overrides.synopsis: Belisarius Cawl turns from his clashes with Fabius Bile and Cadia's legacy toward the Attilan Gap, an unstable region whose collapse could break Indomitus Crusade supply lines. He believes the answer lies in control codes hidden on an ancient Necron tomb world trapped at the edge of a black hole, a place where physics and faith in the Machine-God both start to fray. The novel pushes Cawl into explorer, puzzle-solver, and possible techno-heretic at once. A Mechanicus mystery about why the Imperium keeps needing a genius it cannot trust.

Lieutenant Hadeya Etsul and the battered crew of the Leman Russ Demolisher Steel Tread survived Croatoas once; now their reward is fame, propaganda attention, and transfer into another offensive on the same meat-grinder world. Their tank is patched together, their replacement crew is still raw, and command treats their unlikely victory as proof they can do the impossible again. Demolisher keeps the war inside the hull: cramped tempers, damaged machinery, and a Cadian officer learning that becoming a symbol may be deadlier than being forgotten.

Commissar Valian Hesh is stranded on Rezlan VI with orders that run against every instinct of the Death Korps of Krieg: find the 472nd Siege Regiment and make them retreat before Exterminatus falls. The regiment is dug in against the T'au Empire and preparing the kind of suicidal assault its soldiers were born to consider holy. Hesh has to argue duty against duty while poison skies close over the battlefield and an Imperial warship counts down overhead. A bleak Krieg pressure-cooker about obedience, sacrifice, and the terror of surviving when doctrine demands death.

flags.reason: Roster authors[] empty; canonical author confirmed by Black Library + Lexicanum + Goonhammer + Warhammer-Community + Archeron-Games

overrides.synopsis: The Green Tide gathers an Ork-front anthology around two full novels, two novellas, and four short stories, from Warboss and Catachan Devil to Iron Resolve, Prisoners of Waaagh!, and smaller tales of painboyz, mad doks, warp trouble, and enemies forced into the same killing ground. Its common question is brutally simple: how do you fight a species that treats war as appetite, sport, politics, religion, and punchline all at once? The collection ranges from Ork succession brawls to Catachan jungle warfare and Imperial desperation, keeping the tone loud, mean, and weirdly joyful whenever the greenskins get the last laugh.

flags.reason: Multi-author Ork-themed anthology; roster authors[] empty. Confirmed contributors via Simon-&-Schuster + Lexicanum + Black Library product page + Noble Knight Games product page: Mike Brooks (Warboss), Justin Woolley (Catachan Devil + Prisoners of Waaagh!), Steve Lyons (Iron Resolve), Graham McNeill (The Enemy of My Enemy), additional short-story contributors per Black-Library product page.

overrides.synopsis: Inquisitor Jaq Draco of the Ordo Malleus begins with a covert watch over a Genestealer purge and finds a deeper heresy moving inside the Imperium itself. The trail leads to the Hydra, a rogue Inquisition cabal chasing mind-slavery as salvation and dragging Draco toward the same corruption it claims to defeat. Around him gather the trilogy's strange retinue: Meh'Lindi the Callidus Assassin, Vitali Googol the Navigator, and Grimm the Squat engineer. The first Warhammer 40,000 novel reads like feverish Inquisition noir, half founding text and half early-canon hallucination.

A century after Inquisitor, Jaq Draco is a renegade, hunted by the Inquisition and still chasing the Hydra conspiracy that may enslave humanity in order to save it. The trail runs through the devastated world of Stalinvast, where Eldar Harlequins prepare a Ceremony of Cataclysm on an orbital theatre and Space Marines move to purge them before their secrets escape. Harlequin brings the Black Library of Chaos and the Book of the Rhana Dandra onto the board. The middle volume is a strange chase through guilt, alien theatre, and Inquisitorial self-betrayal.

Chaos Child closes the Inquisition War with Draco's surviving retinue fleeing through the Webway, chasing the Hydra conspiracy and the Book of the Rhana Dandra toward Sabulorb and beyond. Thousand Sons enter the hunt for the stolen lore, while the old certainties around the Emperor, the Webway, and humanity's soul keep buckling into early-40K cosmological weirdness. The ending breaks the retinue, sends some into death or madness, and leaves Draco in a final state of enlightenment that feels more like a cliff edge than closure. A haunted, unresolved finale to the setting's founding trilogy.
